problem_spec.description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

variables.name: base_SMILES
variables.description: Base encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: ligand_SMILES
variables.description: Ligand encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: solvent_SMILES
variables.description: Solvent encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: concentration
variables.description: Discrete concentration levels observed in the dataset

variables.name: temperature
variables.description: Discrete temperature levels observed in the dataset

constraints: Only propose conditions that correspond to rows present in the DAR dataset.
problem_spec.raw_description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

kernel_config.rationale: Selected Matern52 kernel over alternatives due to the 15-iteration catastrophic failure of mixed_sum_product kernel [OBS:E14-E29]. The mixed_sum_product kernel's multiplicative combination of continuous and categorical kernels created numerical instability with 29 observations and 26 categorical levels, causing repeated ModelFittingErrors and forcing random fallback selection. Matern52 provides a simpler, smoother covariance structure that is more robust to limited data and sharp categorical transitions (e.g., 0% to 77% yield across solvent/anion boundaries). The Matern52 kernel with automatic relevance determination per dimension can adapt to the varying importance of the 5 categorical variables (ligand, base, solvent, concentration, temperature) without requiring explicit mixed-space kernel composition. This is a conservative, stability-first choice given the GP's recent failure history [CONFIG:v2, v3 both failed]. Confidence: 0.85 [RULE:R14: prioritize reconfiguration when performance stagnant].

bo_config.rationale: Selected UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) with beta=2.0 over default log_ei due to the 11-iteration stagnation since the 83.83% optimum [OBS:E18]. The campaign has been stuck at 83.83% for iterations 19-29 with recent catastrophic failures (0%, 0%, 4.74%) indicating the surrogate has lost predictive accuracy. UCB provides explicit exploration-exploitation control via the beta parameter, which is critical for this reconfiguration phase. Beta=2.0 balances: (1) exploitation of the known 83.83% optimum region (tert-butyl-Xantphos/pivalate/butyronitrile); (2) exploration of high-uncertainty regions like BINAP/dppf scaffolds and concentration-temperature boundaries per [HYPOTHESIS:H3, H4]. This is more aggressive than log_ei would allow, appropriate given the need to break stagnation [RULE:R14: stronger exploration needed]. Confidence: 0.80.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich dienophiles paired with electron-deficient dienes will maximize Diels-Alder reaction yield due to complementary frontier molecular orbital (FMO) interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: The Diels-Alder reaction is a [4+2] cycloaddition where HOMO-LUMO gap minimization between diene and dienophile accelerates the reaction. Electron-rich dienophiles (high HOMO) and electron-deficient dienes (low LUMO) create favorable orbital overlap, lowering activation energy and increasing yield.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Combinations with dienophiles bearing electron-donating groups (EDGs) and dienes with electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) will show systematically higher yields than mismatched electronic pairs.

hypotheses.text: Steric hindrance at the diene termini will reduce reaction yield by impeding the required s-cis conformation and approach trajectory.
hypotheses.mechanism: The Diels-Alder transition state requires the diene to adopt an s-cis conformation and approach the dienophile in a specific orientation. Bulky substituents at the diene termini increase the energy barrier for achieving this geometry, reducing reaction efficiency.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Dienes with bulky substituents at C1/C4 positions will exhibit lower yields compared to unhindered or internally-substituted dienes, independent of electronic effects.

hypotheses.text: Catalysts with Lewis acidic metal centers will enhance yield by coordinating to polarized functional groups on the dienophile, lowering LUMO energy.
hypotheses.mechanism: Lewis acid catalysts coordinate to electron-withdrawing groups on the dienophile, further depleting electron density and lowering the LUMO energy. This reduces the HOMO(diene)-LUMO(dienophile) gap, accelerating the cycloaddition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Catalysts with stronger Lewis acidity (e.g., Al-based vs. Zn-based) will show higher yields when paired with carbonyl-containing dienophiles.

hypotheses.text: Solvent polarity will positively correlate with yield for normal electron-demand Diels-Alder reactions by stabilizing the polar transition state.
hypotheses.mechanism: The Diels-Alder transition state has partial charge separation. Polar solvents stabilize this developing charge distribution through solvation, lowering activation energy. This effect is more pronounced for normal electron-demand systems.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Higher dielectric constant solvents will produce higher yields for normal electron-demand DAR, with the effect diminishing for reactions with minimal charge separation in the TS.

hypotheses.text: There exist synergistic ternary combinations of diene-dienophile-catalyst that produce yields significantly higher than predicted from pairwise additive effects.
hypotheses.mechanism: Non-linear interactions between components can create cooperative effects where the catalyst optimally orients both diene and dienophile, or where specific electronic matching creates resonance stabilization in the transition state that is not present in binary combinations.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Certain three-component combinations will yield >20% above the average of their respective pairwise combinations, indicating positive cooperativity.

hypotheses.text: Bidentate phosphine ligands with large bite angles (Xantphos-type) are strictly required for high DAR yields; monodentate phosphines are fundamentally incompatible regardless of steric/electronic tuning.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR mechanism requires simultaneous coordination of both the diene and dienophile to the metal center for proper orbital alignment. Bidentate phosphines enforce a rigid coordination geometry that positions substrates optimally for [4+2] cycloaddition. Monodentate phosphines allow flexible coordination that fails to pre-organize substrates, leading to unproductive catalytic cycles. The Xantphos backbone provides the optimal bite angle (~111°) for this specific transformation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: All monodentate phosphines (t-Bu3P, t-Bu2PPh, PPh3, PCy3) will yield <10% regardless of solvent, while bidentate phosphines (Xantphos, BINAP, dppf, DPEphos) will yield >30% under optimized conditions (KOAc, p-xylene or butyronitrile, 120°C, 0.1-0.153 M).

hypotheses.text: Solvent polarity operates through a threshold mechanism: non-polar aromatic (p-xylene) and moderately polar aprotic (butyronitrile) enable high yields, while strongly coordinating polar aprotic solvents (DMF) poison the catalyst regardless of other variables.
hypotheses.mechanism: The active catalytic species requires a coordinatively unsaturated metal center for substrate binding. p-Xylene is non-coordinating and butyronitrile binds weakly, preserving catalytic activity. DMF is a strong σ-donor that displaces the bidentate phosphine or substrates from the metal coordination sphere, forming catalytically inactive adducts. This is a thermodynamic poisoning effect, not kinetic.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing additional solvents across the polarity scale: THF (moderate polarity, moderate coordination) will yield intermediate results (20-40%); acetonitrile (similar to butyronitrile) will match butyronitrile performance (~55%); DMSO (strong donor, like DMF) will yield <5%. The threshold between viable and poisoned solvents correlates with Gutmann donor number, not dielectric constant alone.

hypotheses.text: The Xantphos/KOAc cooperative catalytic system exhibits ligand-specific activity that does not generalize to other bidentate phosphine scaffolds (BINAP, dppf, DPEphos) without re-optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 61.67% yield achieved with Xantphos reflects a specific match between the xanthene backbone geometry, dicyclohexylphosphino electronic properties, and KOAc base strength. Alternative bidentate scaffolds have different bite angles (BINAP ~92°, dppf ~99°, DPEphos ~104° vs. Xantphos ~111°) and electronic properties that may require different bases (Cs2CO3, K3PO4) or solvents for optimal performance. The cooperative effect is scaffold-dependent.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Direct substitution of Xantphos with BINAP, dppf, or DPEphos under identical conditions (KOAc, p-xylene, 120°C, 0.153 M) will yield <40%. However, each alternative scaffold will achieve >50% yields when paired with its optimal base-solvent combination, requiring 3-5 iterations per scaffold to identify conditions.

hypotheses.text: Temperature effects are supra-linear in the Xantphos/KOAc/p-xylene system: yield increases accelerate above 105°C due to favorable entropy of activation and absence of product decomposition pathways.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR transition state has a highly ordered geometry with significant entropic penalty. Higher temperatures disproportionately favor the transition state over reactants (ΔS‡ > 0). Additionally, the Xantphos-ligated catalyst is thermally robust—no phosphine dissociation or decomposition occurs up to at least 135°C. The accelerating yield trend (28.36% → 39.32% → 61.67%) reflects this entropic driving force, not just Arrhenius kinetics.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing 135°C and 150°C with Xantphos/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M will yield >70% and potentially >80% respectively. The yield-temperature relationship will show positive second derivative (concave up) up to a thermal decomposition threshold >150°C. Testing 75°C will yield <15%, confirming the non-linear temperature dependence.

hypotheses.text: Mixed-kernel Bayesian optimization with explicit categorical encoding for solvents will restore model predictive accuracy and enable efficient exploration of the bidentate phosphine scaffold space.
hypotheses.mechanism: The GP failure stems from treating discrete solvent categories (p-xylene, butyronitrile, DMF) as continuous variables in fingerprint space, causing kernel lengthscale mismatch. A sum kernel combining (1) Tanimoto kernel for ligand scaffold similarity, (2) RBF/Matern for continuous variables (concentration, temperature), and (3) explicit categorical/one-hot encoding for solvents will correctly model the discontinuous yield surface. This structural kernel respects the chemical heterogeneity of the search space.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to mixed-kernel BO, the model will (a) correctly predict near-zero yields for DMF-based candidates, (b) predict 50-65% yields for butyronitrile/p-xylene with bidentate phosphines, and (c) generate shortlists that prioritize untested bidentate scaffolds (BINAP, dppf) over redundant solvent/ligand combinations. Model uncertainty will be well-calibrated (predicted uncertainty matches observed error).

hypotheses.text: Bidentate phosphine ligands with large bite angles (Xantphos-type) are strictly required for high DAR yields; monodentate phosphines are fundamentally incompatible regardless of steric/electronic tuning.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR mechanism requires simultaneous coordination of both the diene and dienophile to the metal center for proper orbital alignment. Bidentate phosphines enforce a rigid coordination geometry that positions substrates optimally for [4+2] cycloaddition. Monodentate phosphines allow flexible coordination that fails to pre-organize substrates, leading to unproductive catalytic cycles. The Xantphos backbone provides the optimal bite angle (~111°) for this specific transformation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: All monodentate phosphines (t-Bu3P, t-Bu2PPh, PPh3, PCy3) will yield <10% regardless of solvent, while bidentate phosphines (Xantphos, BINAP, dppf, DPEphos) will yield >30% under optimized conditions (KOAc, p-xylene or butyronitrile, 120°C, 0.1-0.153 M).

hypotheses.text: The Xantphos/KOAc/butyronitrile system at 0.1 M/120°C represents a viable alternative to the p-xylene/0.153 M optimum, with yield penalty <10% absolute.
hypotheses.mechanism: Butyronitrile (ε~20) is moderately polar but less coordinating than DMF. At 120°C, thermal energy overcomes the modest solvent polarity penalty, allowing the Xantphos/KOAc cooperative catalytic cycle to proceed efficiently. The 0.1 M concentration reduces mass transfer limitations without catastrophically diluting reactive intermediates. This creates a robust 'medium-polarity' operating window distinct from the strict non-polar requirement initially hypothesized.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing Xantphos/KOAc/butyronitrile at 0.153 M/120°C will achieve 55-65% yield, statistically indistinguishable from the p-xylene optimum (61.67%). Testing at 135°C will achieve >65% yield, potentially exceeding the current best.

hypotheses.text: The 61.67% yield at 120°C is near the thermal optimum; further temperature increases to 135°C will show diminishing returns (<5% absolute improvement) or yield decline due to catalyst decomposition.
hypotheses.mechanism: The accelerating yield trend (28.36% → 39.32% → 61.67%) suggests the system is approaching a kinetic ceiling. At 135°C, competing pathways (retro-Diels-Alder, phosphine oxidation, metal colloid formation) will begin to dominate, offsetting the rate enhancement from increased thermal energy. The Xantphos ligand's thermal stability limit is approximately 130-140°C under these conditions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M at 135°C will yield 60-68% (plateau or slight increase). At 150°C, yield will decline to <50% due to catalyst degradation.

hypotheses.text: Alternative bidentate phosphine scaffolds (BINAP, dppf, DPEphos) will achieve >50% yield with KOAc/p-xylene/120°C, confirming that high yields are not Xantphos-specific but generalizable to bidentate architectures.
hypotheses.mechanism: The critical feature enabling high yields is the chelate effect and bite angle control, not the specific Xantphos xanthene backbone. BINAP (atropisomeric biaryl, bite angle ~92°), dppf (ferrocenyl spacer, bite angle ~99°), and DPEphos (ether linkage, bite angle ~104°) all provide rigid bidentate coordination that pre-organizes substrates. Each will exhibit distinct optimal temperatures due to electronic and steric differences, but all will surpass the monodentate ceiling.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M/120°C will yield 45-60%; dppf/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M/120°C will yield 50-65%; DPEphos/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M/120°C will yield 40-55%. At least one alternative scaffold will achieve >55% yield.

hypotheses.text: DMF is uniquely incompatible with this DAR system due to competitive metal coordination that displaces the phosphine ligand, whereas other polar solvents (butyronitrile, THF, acetone) may be tolerated at elevated temperatures.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF's strong donor properties (high Gutmann donor number, 26.6) enable it to displace even bidentate phosphines from the metal center, forming catalytically inactive solvated species. Butyronitrile is a weaker donor (DN ~15) and less effective at ligand displacement. The 56.57% yield with butyronitrile (Iteration 9) versus 0-5% with DMF (Iterations 1-4, 8, 10) reflects this coordination chemistry difference, not merely polarity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing Xantphos/KOAc/THF/0.153 M/120°C will yield 30-50%; testing with acetone will yield 20-40%; testing with NMP (similar to DMF) will yield <10%. The donor number threshold for compatibility is approximately 15-18.

hypotheses.text: Bidentate phosphine ligands are required for high DAR yields; the specific chelation mode (rigid vs. hemilabile) determines solvent compatibility rather than yield ceiling.
hypotheses.mechanism: Both rigid bidentates (Xantphos-type, bite angle ~111°) and hemilabile bidentates (JohnPhos-type with ortho-dimethylamino coordination) enforce the coordination geometry necessary for substrate pre-organization. However, hemilabile ligands can dynamically dissociate/associate the weakly-coordinating amine arm, creating transient coordination sites that tolerate polar solvents like DMF. Rigid bidentates maintain fixed coordination spheres that are disrupted by competitive polar solvent coordination, causing catalyst deactivation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos and related hemilabile biaryl phosphines (DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) will achieve >60% yield in DMF, while Xantphos-type ligands will remain <10% in DMF. Both ligand classes will achieve >70% in non-polar solvents (p-xylene, butyronitrile).

hypotheses.text: Polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMAc, NMP) are compatible with DAR when paired with hemilabile bidentate phosphines and K+ base at 0.1 M/105°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 78.95% yield with JohnPhos/DMF overturns the prior 0% DMF history. The key difference is ligand hemilability: the ortho-dimethylamino group in JohnPhos provides a labile coordination site that can temporarily dissociate under polar solvent competition, then re-coordinate to stabilize the active catalyst. K+ (vs. Cs+) may enhance this effect through different ion-pairing with the acetate base, affecting the catalytic cycle dynamics.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing JohnPhos with DMAc (CC(=O)N(C)C) and NMP (CN1CCCC1=O) at 0.1 M/105°C with K-acetate will achieve >60% yield. Testing Xantphos with DMF will remain <10%, confirming ligand-specific solvent compatibility.

hypotheses.text: The tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile system at 0.1 M/105°C represents a near-global optimum for rigid bidentate phosphines.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 83.83% yield at iteration 18 is the campaign maximum. The system shows robustness across modest condition variations (Cs/K, pivalate/acetate, 0.057-0.1 M, 105-120°C) but fails at high concentration/low temperature (0% at 0.153 M/90°C). The concentration-temperature coupling suggests mass transport or solubility limitations: butyronitrile requires >100°C to maintain homogeneous conditions at >0.12 M.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Further optimization of tert-butyl-Xantphos will yield <5% improvement (ceiling ~88%). The next yield breakthrough requires either: (a) hemilabile ligands in polar solvents (JohnPhos/DMF pathway), or (b) novel bidentate scaffolds (BINAP, dppf, DPEphos) with optimized conditions.

hypotheses.text: BINAP, dppf, and DPEphos will achieve >60% yield with optimized base/solvent combinations, but each requires scaffold-specific condition tuning.
hypotheses.mechanism: The tert-butyl-Xantphos success validates that non-xanthene bidentate architectures can achieve high yields, but the 10-iteration gap without testing BINAP/dppf/DPEphos leaves major chemical space unexplored. Each scaffold has distinct bite angles (BINAP ~92°, dppf ~99°, DPEphos ~104°) and electronic properties that will shift optimal conditions. BINAP's atropisomeric biaryl may tolerate polar solvents similarly to JohnPhos; dppf's ferrocenyl flexibility may require different temperature tuning.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/K-acetate/DMF/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 55-70% (biaryl scaffold + polar solvent compatibility). Dppf/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 50-65% (ferrocenyl flexibility benefits from Cs+). DPEphos/KOAc/p-xylene/0.153 M/120°C will achieve 45-60% (ether linkage prefers non-polar).

hypotheses.text: GP model instability stems from the mixed_sum_product kernel's complexity with limited data (n=19) and categorical variable imbalance (DMF underrepresented due to prior 0% history).
hypotheses.mechanism: The ModelFittingError at iteration 19 indicates numerical instability. The mixed_sum_product kernel combines continuous (concentration, temperature) and categorical (SMILES-encoded) features with product interactions. With only 19 data points and severe class imbalance (DMF: 6 failures, 1 success; p-xylene/butyronitrile: multiple successes), the kernel hyperparameters cannot be reliably optimized. The JohnPhos/DMF success is an outlier that the model cannot accommodate.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to a simpler kernel (Matern52 with explicit categorical encoding or Tanimoto for ligand similarity) will restore model stability. Adding 3-5 more diverse observations (BINAP, dppf, DPEphos tests) will improve kernel hyperparameter estimation and prevent fallback.

hypotheses.text: Hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) uniquely enable high DAR yields in polar aprotic solvents (DMF, NMP, DMSO) through dynamic coordination, while rigid bidentates (Xantphos, BINAP, dppf) are poisoned by polar solvents.
hypotheses.mechanism: Hemilabile ligands contain a strongly-coordinating phosphine and a weakly-coordinating heteroatom (amine, ether) that can reversibly dissociate. In polar solvents, the weakly-coordinating arm temporarily dissociates, creating transient coordination sites for substrate binding while the strongly-bound phosphine maintains catalyst integrity. Rigid bidentates cannot dynamically adjust their coordination sphere, making them vulnerable to competitive solvent coordination that disrupts the catalytic cycle.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos, DavePhos, RuPhos, and SPhos will achieve >60% yield in DMF, while Xantphos, BINAP, and dppf will achieve <10% in DMF. In non-polar solvents (p-xylene, butyronitrile), both ligand classes will achieve >70% yield.

hypotheses.text: The optimal ligand-solvent pairing follows a chelation-polarity matching principle: hemilabile ligands for polar solvents, rigid bidentates for non-polar solvents.
hypotheses.mechanism: The coordination flexibility of hemilabile ligands is advantageous in polar solvents where solvent competition is high, but unnecessary in non-polar solvents where rigid chelation provides optimal geometric control. Conversely, rigid bidentates achieve maximum enantioselectivity and activity in non-polar media but fail in polar solvents due to irreversible solvent coordination.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/DMF and Xantphos/p-xylene will both achieve >75% yield, but JohnPhos/p-xylene and Xantphos/DMF will achieve <50% yield. Cross-pairing performance will be systematically inferior to matched pairing.

hypotheses.text: Concentration-temperature coupling is solvent-dependent: polar solvents require lower concentrations (<0.1 M) across all temperatures, while non-polar solvents tolerate higher concentrations (0.1-0.15 M) at elevated temperatures (>100°C).
hypotheses.mechanism: Polar solvents have higher dielectric constants and stronger solvation effects that can disrupt catalyst-substrate interactions at high concentrations. Non-polar solvents provide weaker solvation, allowing higher substrate/catalyst densities without competitive inhibition. The 0% yield at 0.153 M/90°C in butyronitrile suggests even non-polar solvents have concentration limits at suboptimal temperatures.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/DMF will achieve >70% at 0.057-0.1 M but <30% at 0.153 M, regardless of temperature. Xantphos/p-xylene will achieve >70% at 0.1-0.153 M/105-120°C but <40% at 0.153 M/90°C.

hypotheses.text: Cs+ cation provides superior performance with hemilabile ligands in polar solvents due to enhanced transmetalation kinetics and ion pairing, while K+ is optimal for rigid bidentates in non-polar solvents.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cs+ has larger ionic radius and weaker Lewis acidity, creating looser ion pairs that facilitate transmetalation in polar media where solvent-separated ions dominate. K+ forms tighter ion pairs that stabilize the catalytic intermediate in non-polar solvents where contact ion pairs are prevalent. The tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs+ success in butyronitrile suggests this effect is ligand-dependent rather than strictly solvent-dependent.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/DMF with Cs+ will achieve >75% yield, while JohnPhos/DMF with K+ will achieve <65%. Xantphos/p-xylene with K+ will achieve >75%, while with Cs+ will achieve <70%.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield ceiling is determined by ligand scaffold modularity rather than specific substituents: xanthene, biaryl, and ferrocenyl backbones can all achieve >80% yields when paired with compatible solvents.
hypotheses.mechanism: The chelation geometry (bite angle, flexibility) is the primary determinant of catalytic activity, while backbone identity (xanthene vs. biphenyl vs. ferrocene) provides modular platforms for tuning steric and electronic properties. Once optimal conditions are identified for a scaffold class, yield optimization focuses on substituent tuning rather than backbone replacement.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos (biaryl), RuPhos (biaryl), and dppf (ferrocenyl) will all achieve >70% yields in their respective optimal solvent systems (DMF for biaryls, p-xylene for ferrocenyls). Systematic optimization of each scaffold can reach >80% yields.

hypotheses.text: Bidentate phosphine ligands are required for high DAR yields (>60%); the specific chelation mode (rigid vs. hemilabile) determines solvent compatibility rather than yield ceiling.
hypotheses.mechanism: Both rigid bidentates (Xantphos-type, bite angle ~111°) and hemilabile bidentates (JohnPhos-type with ortho-dimethylamino coordination) enforce the coordination geometry necessary for substrate pre-organization. However, hemilabile ligands can dynamically dissociate/associate the weakly-coordinating amine arm, creating transient coordination sites that tolerate polar solvents like DMF. Rigid bidentates maintain fixed coordination spheres that are disrupted by competitive polar solvent coordination, causing catalyst deactivation. Monodentate phosphines lack the chelate effect entirely and fail regardless of conditions [OBS:E1-E13].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos and related hemilabile biaryl phosphines (DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) will achieve >60% yield in DMF, while Xantphos-type ligands will remain <10% in DMF. Both ligand classes will achieve >70% in non-polar solvents (p-xylene, butyronitrile).

hypotheses.text: Alternative bidentate scaffolds (BINAP, dppf, DPEphos, DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) will achieve >60% yield when paired with compatible solvents, confirming that high yields are not Xantphos-specific or JohnPhos-specific but generalizable to appropriate ligand architectures.
hypotheses.mechanism: The critical feature enabling high yields is the chelate effect and appropriate coordination flexibility for the solvent polarity, not the specific backbone identity. BINAP (rigid biaryl, bite angle ~92°), dppf (ferrocenyl, bite angle ~99°), DPEphos (ether-linked, bite angle ~104°), and the hemilabile phosphines (DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) all provide bidentate coordination. Each will exhibit distinct optimal solvents based on their flexibility: rigid scaffolds for non-polar, hemilabile for polar.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/K-acetate/DMF/0.1 M/105°C will yield >65%; BINAP/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will yield >55%; dppf/Cs-acetate/p-xylene/0.1 M/120°C will yield >50%. At least two alternative scaffolds will achieve >60% yield in their optimal solvent systems.

hypotheses.text: Base cation effects (K+ vs. Cs+) are secondary to ligand-solvent matching: both cations can achieve >70% yields when paired with appropriate ligand-solvent combinations.
hypotheses.mechanism: The tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile system achieved 72.89% [OBS:E15], while JohnPhos/K-acetate/DMF achieved 78.95% [OBS:E19]. The 83.83% optimum uses Cs-acetate [OBS:E18]. This suggests cation selection is tunable rather than deterministic—Cs+ may enhance rigid bidentate performance, while K+ may be preferred for hemilabile systems, but neither is strictly required or prohibited.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/DMF will achieve >75% yield (improving on 78.95% with K+). Xantphos/K-pivalate/butyronitrile will achieve >65% yield (vs. 72.89% with Cs+). The yield variation from cation swapping will be <10% absolute when ligand-solvent pairing is optimal.

hypotheses.text: Hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, DavePhos, RuPhos, SPhos) uniquely enable high DAR yields (>70%) in polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMAc, NMP) through dynamic ligand dissociation/reassociation that creates transient coordination sites compatible with polar solvent competition.
hypotheses.mechanism: The ortho-dimethylamino (or alkoxy) substituent on the biaryl backbone provides a hemilabile N→Pd coordination that can reversibly dissociate under polar solvent competition. This dynamic behavior maintains catalytic activity by allowing substrate access while the chelate effect prevents complete ligand loss. Rigid bidentates (Xantphos, BINAP, dppf) cannot dissociate and are poisoned by polar solvent coordination that blocks substrate binding [OBS:E19, E20]. The bite angle (~90°) and electron-rich Pd center from the dialkylphosphine further stabilize the active species.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos-type ligands will achieve >70% yield in DMF with Cs/K carboxylate bases at 0.05-0.15 M/100-110°C. Xantphos/BINAP/dppf in identical conditions will achieve <20% yield. DavePhos (ortho-OMe) and RuPhos (ortho-iPr) will show similar DMF compatibility with yield variations based on steric/electronic tuning.

hypotheses.text: Rigid bidentate phosphines (Xantphos-type, BINAP, dppf) achieve maximum DAR yields (>80%) exclusively in non-polar solvents (p-xylene, butyronitrile, toluene) where the fixed chelation geometry enforces optimal coordination sphere without polar solvent competition.
hypotheses.mechanism: The rigid chelate structure (xanthene backbone bite angle ~111°, BINAP ~92°, dppf ~99°) pre-organizes the Pd center for C-H activation but requires a non-coordinating environment. Polar solvents (DMF, DMSO) compete for Pd coordination and cannot be displaced by the rigid ligand framework, causing catalyst deactivation. Non-polar solvents allow exclusive ligand coordination, maximizing turnover [OBS:E18: 83.83% with tert-butyl-Xantphos/butyronitrile].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos with Cs-acetate will achieve 80-85% yield in butyronitrile or p-xylene at 0.08-0.12 M/100-110°C. The same system in DMF will achieve <30% yield. BINAP and dppf will show similar solvent-dependent performance with optimal yields in non-polar media and failure in polar media.

hypotheses.text: Concentration and temperature exhibit strong positive coupling in butyronitrile solvent: high concentration (>0.12 M) requires high temperature (>100°C) to maintain homogeneous reaction conditions and catalytic activity, while low concentration tolerates broader temperature ranges.
hypotheses.mechanism: At 0.153 M in butyronitrile, the reaction mixture may approach saturation or exhibit increased viscosity at lower temperatures, causing mass transfer limitations or catalyst aggregation. Higher temperatures (105-120°C) maintain fluidity and molecular dispersion. At 0.057 M, the system remains dilute enough that temperature effects are dominated by kinetic rather than physical factors [OBS:E17: 0% at 0.153 M/90°C vs. 72%+ at 0.057 M/105-120°C].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile at 0.15 M will achieve >70% yield only at 105-120°C, and <20% yield at 90°C. At 0.06 M, the same system will achieve 70-80% yield across 90-120°C. The concentration-temperature interaction will be less pronounced in p-xylene due to better solubility characteristics.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf represent unexplored bidentate scaffolds that may achieve comparable or superior yields to Xantphos-type ligands in non-polar solvents due to different bite angles and electronic properties, but will similarly fail in polar solvents due to rigid chelation.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (bite angle ~92°, atropisomeric backbone) and dppf (bite angle ~99°, ferrocenyl backbone) offer distinct coordination geometries and electronic environments compared to Xantphos (~111°). These may better accommodate specific substrate geometries or transmetalation transition states. However, both retain rigid bidentate character and will be subject to the same polar solvent poisoning mechanism as Xantphos [HYPOTHESIS:H2].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP with Cs-acetate in butyronitrile/p-xylene at 0.1 M/105°C will achieve 75-85% yield, potentially matching or exceeding tert-butyl-Xantphos. dppf under identical conditions will achieve 70-80% yield. Both ligands in DMF will achieve <15% yield, confirming rigid bidentate incompatibility with polar solvents regardless of scaffold.

hypotheses.text: The GP model fitting failures (iterations 18-20) stem from the mixed_sum_product kernel's excessive expressiveness relative to the 20-observation dataset, causing numerical instability; switching to a simpler Matern52 kernel with automatic relevance determination will restore stable BO functionality.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel combines additive and multiplicative interactions across 5 categorical variables with 26 total categories, creating a high-dimensional hyperparameter space. With only 20 observations, the marginal likelihood surface becomes flat or multimodal, causing optimization failure and Cholesky decomposition errors. A simpler Matern52 kernel with explicit categorical encoding reduces dimensionality and improves conditioning [OBS:ModelFittingError at iterations 18, 19, 20].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguring to Matern52 kernel with 5-dimensional one-hot encoding will enable successful GP fitting on the 20-observation dataset with Cholesky decomposition success and meaningful uncertainty estimates. Acquisition function optimization will produce chemically sensible candidates (no DMF/monodentate combinations) rather than random fallback. Predictive performance on held-out validation points will improve from failed to RMSE <15% yield.

hypotheses.text: Hemilabile bidentate phosphines require N-donor hemilability (dimethylamino) to achieve high yields (>70%) in polar aprotic solvents; O-donor hemilability (methoxy) is insufficient due to weaker Pd coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: The N→Pd dative bond in JohnPhos-type ligands (dimethylamino substituents) is significantly stronger than the O→Pd bond in DavePhos-type ligands (methoxy substituents). In DMF competition, the N-donor maintains sufficient coordination to preserve catalyst integrity, while the O-donor dissociates irreversibly, leaving Pd vulnerable to DMF poisoning [OBS:E19: 78.95% JohnPhos/DMF vs. OBS:E21: 0.07% DavePhos/DMF]. The donor atom determines hemilabile effectiveness: nitrogen's stronger σ-donation and better orbital overlap with Pd create a hemilability window that balances lability for catalytic turnover with stability against solvent displacement.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl phosphine) will achieve >65% yield in DMF at 0.1 M/105°C, while SPhos (methoxy-biaryl) will achieve <10% in identical conditions. Testing SPhos will further validate the N>O donor hierarchy. No O-hemilabile ligand will exceed 15% yield in DMF.

hypotheses.text: Rigid bidentate phosphines (Xantphos, BINAP, dppf) achieve maximum DAR yields (>80%) exclusively in non-polar solvents; polar solvent tolerance is uniquely conferred by N-hemilability, not achievable through rigid chelation optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: Rigid bidentates rely on fixed bite angles and chelate stability that cannot accommodate polar solvent competition. The 83.83% optimum with tert-butyl-Xantphos/butyronitrile [OBS:E18] represents the ceiling for rigid systems in moderately polar media. DMF (stronger donor, ε~37) displaces even rigid bidentates. Only hemilabile ligands with N-donors can dynamically respond to solvent competition through reversible coordination. This creates a strict ligand-solvent compatibility matrix: rigid→non-polar, N-hemilabile→polar, O-hemilabile→neither.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP and dppf in butyronitrile or p-xylene will achieve 75-85% yield, but <10% in DMF. Xantphos in DMF will remain <15% regardless of base or conditions. The 83.83% tert-butyl-Xantphos/butyronitrile result is near the rigid bidentate ceiling.

hypotheses.text: The tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile system at 0.1 M/105°C (83.83% yield) represents a robust optimum with broad condition tolerance; small perturbations (±0.02 M, ±10°C, K+ vs. Cs+) will maintain >75% yield.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 83.83% yield at iteration 18 suggests a flat optimum in the concentration-temperature space around 0.1 M/105°C. The concentration-temperature coupling observed at 0.153 M/90°C [OBS:E17] is avoided at 0.1 M. The system exhibits 'platform behavior' where the chelation geometry (tert-butyl-Xantphos bite angle ~111°), solvent polarity (butyronitrile ε~20), and thermal energy (105°C) create a cooperative catalytic environment insensitive to minor variations.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing tert-butyl-Xantphos with K-acetate (vs. Cs-acetate) in butyronitrile at 0.1 M/105°C will achieve 78-85% yield. Testing at 0.08 M/105°C and 0.12 M/105°C will both achieve >75%. Testing at 95°C and 115°C will maintain >75%. The optimum is a broad plateau, not a sharp peak.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf represent high-priority unexplored scaffolds that may match or exceed Xantphos performance in non-polar solvents through distinct bite angles and electronic tuning.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (bite angle ~92°, atropisomeric biaryl) and dppf (bite angle ~99°, ferrocenyl backbone) offer coordination geometries distinct from Xantphos (~111°). These may better accommodate the DAR transition state geometry or transmetalation kinetics. The ferrocenyl spacer in dppf provides electronic flexibility through the iron center; BINAP's atropisomeric backbone creates a chiral environment even with racemic material. Both are established in cross-coupling but untested in this DAR system.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile at 0.1 M/105°C will achieve 80-88% yield, potentially exceeding the current 83.83% best. dppf under identical conditions will achieve 75-82% yield. Both will show the same solvent specificity as Xantphos: high yields in butyronitrile/p-xylene, failure in DMF.

hypotheses.text: Matern52 kernel with ARD (automatic relevance determination) will restore stable GP modeling, enabling BO to exploit the 83.83% optimum and explore BINAP/dppf efficiently without random fallback.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel's complexity (additive+multiplicative interactions across 5 categorical variables with 26 categories) exceeds the information content of 21 observations, causing hyperparameter optimization failure and Cholesky decomposition errors [OBS:ModelFittingError iterations 18-21]. Matern52 with ARD reduces dimensionality by learning lengthscales per variable, providing sufficient expressiveness for yield surface modeling while maintaining numerical stability. The 21 observations now include critical structure: polar/non-polar solvent distinction, N-hemilabile vs. rigid bidentate vs. monodentate ligand classes, and concentration-temperature coupling patterns.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Post-reconfiguration, the GP will (a) fit successfully on 21 observations without Cholesky errors, (b) predict <15% yield for DavePhos/DMF and >70% for JohnPhos/DMF, (c) generate shortlists prioritizing BINAP/dppf in butyronitrile and RuPhos in DMF, (d) produce well-calibrated uncertainties (predicted vs. observed error ratio 0.8-1.2). No random fallback will occur for iterations 22-25.

hypotheses.text: Hemilabile bidentate phosphines require N-donor hemilability (dimethylamino) to achieve high yields (>70%) in polar aprotic solvents; O-donor hemilability (methoxy) is insufficient due to weaker Pd coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: The N→Pd dative bond in JohnPhos-type ligands (dimethylamino substituents) is significantly stronger than the O→Pd bond in DavePhos-type ligands (methoxy substituents). In DMF competition, the N-donor maintains sufficient coordination to preserve catalyst integrity, while the O-donor dissociates irreversibly, leaving Pd vulnerable to DMF poisoning [OBS:E19: 78.95% JohnPhos/DMF vs. OBS:E21: 0.07% DavePhos/DMF]. The donor atom determines hemilabile effectiveness: nitrogen's stronger σ-donation and better orbital overlap with Pd create a hemilability window that balances lability for catalytic turnover with stability against solvent displacement.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl phosphine) will achieve >65% yield in DMF at 0.1 M/105°C, while SPhos (methoxy-biaryl) will achieve <10% yield under identical conditions. Testing JohnPhos in butyronitrile will achieve >75% yield, confirming the N-donor mechanism is general across polar solvents.

hypotheses.text: The tert-butyl-Xantphos/butyronitrile system (83.83% yield) represents a robust optimum that tolerates modest concentration and temperature variations, enabling process-friendly operating windows.
hypotheses.mechanism: The rigid xanthene backbone with tert-butyl substituents creates a sterically protected, electronically tuned Pd center that is less sensitive to thermal fluctuations and concentration effects than the hemilabile biaryl systems. The bite angle (111°) and steric bulk (cone angle ~270°) create a stable catalytic pocket that maintains activity across 0.057-0.1 M and 90-120°C [OBS:E15-16: 72.89% at 0.057 M/105°C, 72.21% at 0.057 M/120°C; OBS:E18: 83.83% at 0.1 M/105°C]. The failure at 0.153 M/90°C [OBS:E17] defines the boundary: concentration >0.12 M requires temperature >100°C for homogeneous conditions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing tert-butyl-Xantphos at 0.12 M/110°C will achieve >75% yield, confirming the concentration-temperature tradeoff. The system will maintain >70% yield at 0.08 M/100°C, demonstrating a 20°C×0.05 M operable window suitable for scale-up.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf scaffolds will achieve >70% yields in butyronitrile at 105°C with Cs-acetate, challenging the hypothesis that xanthene backbones are uniquely optimal.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (atropisomeric biaryl, bite angle ~92°) and dppf (ferrocenyl, bite angle ~99°) offer alternative bidentate coordination geometries that may better accommodate the DAR transition state than the xanthene scaffold (bite angle ~111°). The 83.83% yield with tert-butyl-Xantphos suggests the reaction is not yet ligand-optimized—different bite angles may enable faster oxidative addition or reductive elimination. BINAP's chiral environment and dppf's flexible ferrocenyl linker provide distinct electronic and steric profiles that could surpass the xanthene performance [HYPOTHESIS:H4 from prior iterations].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP with Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 75-85% yield, potentially exceeding the tert-butyl-Xantphos optimum. Dppf under identical conditions will achieve 65-75% yield, establishing the bite angle sensitivity: dppf's smaller bite angle (~99° vs. 111°) may be suboptimal for this substrate combination.

hypotheses.text: The GP model failure stems from kernel misspecification—mixed_sum_product is unstable with 21 observations and 5 categorical variables; switching to Matern52 with explicit categorical encoding will restore stable BO.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel combines continuous and categorical kernels multiplicatively, creating a complex hyperparameter landscape that becomes ill-conditioned with limited data and high categorical dimensionality (26 categories across 5 variables). The ModelFittingError at iterations 18-21 indicates numerical instability during hyperparameter optimization. The Matern52 kernel with separate length scales per dimension and explicit one-hot encoding for categoricals provides a simpler, more robust covariance structure that avoids the multiplicative interaction terms causing instability [OBS:E18-21: consecutive model failures].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguring to Matern52 with fingerprint_concat embeddings, the GP will successfully fit within 60 seconds and generate acquisition values >0.1 for the next iteration. The uncertainty estimates will be well-calibrated (predicted ±10-15% vs. actual errors), and the model will prioritize exploitation of the 83.83% tert-butyl-Xantphos condition rather than random fallback.

hypotheses.text: JohnPhos in butyronitrile will achieve >80% yield, surpassing its DMF performance and establishing that N-hemilabile ligands are optimal for polar solvents generally, not just DMF specifically.
hypotheses.mechanism: JohnPhos achieved 78.95% in DMF [OBS:E19] despite DMF's historical toxicity to the reaction. Butyronitrile is a superior solvent per [RULE:R4, confidence=0.92] with demonstrated compatibility with bidentate phosphines (83.83% with tert-butyl-Xantphos). The combination of JohnPhos's hemilabile N-donor stabilization with butyronitrile's favorable solvation properties (less coordinating than DMF, better than p-xylene for polar substrates) should create a synergistic high-performance system. The dimethylamino groups provide catalyst stabilization while butyronitrile provides optimal dielectric environment without competitive coordination.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 80-88% yield, potentially exceeding the current 83.83% best. This would establish JohnPhos as the optimal ligand class and butyronitrile as the optimal solvent, directing final optimization toward fine-tuning concentration (0.08-0.12 M) and temperature (100-110°C) within this ligand-solvent combination.

hypotheses.text: N-hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, RuPhos) achieve >75% yields in both polar (DMF) and non-polar (butyronitrile) solvents, while rigid bidentates (Xantphos) are limited to non-polar solvents; the optimal ligand-solvent pairing follows a hemilabile-polar/rigid-nonpolar matching principle.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino hemilability in JohnPhos-type ligands provides dynamic coordination that tolerates polar solvent competition through reversible N→Pd dissociation/reassociation. This enables high yields in DMF (78.95% observed) while maintaining activity in butyronitrile. Rigid bidentates like tert-butyl-Xantphos achieve maximum yields (83.83%) only in non-polar solvents where fixed chelation geometry is preserved without competitive coordination. The hemilabile mechanism creates a broader solvent compatibility window than rigid chelation [OBS:E18, E19, E21, E22].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve >80% yield, exceeding its DMF performance and challenging the rigid Xantphos optimum. RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl) will achieve >70% in both DMF and butyronitrile, confirming N-hemilability generality. Xantphos in DMF will remain <15% regardless of conditions.

hypotheses.text: The Matern52 kernel with fingerprint_concat embedding will restore stable GP fitting and enable chemically sensible BO recommendations, ending the 7-iteration random fallback streak.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel's multiplicative combination of continuous and categorical kernels creates numerical instability with limited data (n=22) and high categorical dimensionality (26 categories). The Matern52 kernel with additive structure, automatic relevance determination per dimension, and SMILES fingerprint concatenation for molecular features provides a simpler, better-conditioned covariance matrix. This avoids the Cholesky decomposition failures and hyperparameter optimization divergence causing ModelFittingError [OBS:E18-E22: consecutive failures].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Post-reconfiguration, the GP will fit successfully within 60 seconds, generating acquisition values >0.1 with well-calibrated uncertainties (predicted ±10-15% vs actual errors). The next shortlist will prioritize exploitation of the 83.83% tert-butyl-Xantphos condition or exploration of JohnPhos/butyronitrile, with no DMF/monodentate combinations.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf scaffolds will achieve >70% yields in butyronitrile with Cs-acetate at 105°C, establishing that the 83.83% optimum is not Xantphos-specific but generalizable to alternative bidentate architectures with bite angles of 92-111°.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR catalytic cycle requires bidentate phosphine coordination for substrate pre-organization, but the specific backbone (xanthene, biaryl, ferrocenyl) is modular. BINAP (bite angle ~92°) and dppf (~99°) offer distinct electronic and steric environments that may match or exceed the Xantphos performance (~111°). The tert-butyl-Xantphos success demonstrates the reaction is ligand-optimizable; alternative scaffolds provide unexplored optimization space for surpassing 83.83% [HYPOTHESIS:H4 from prior iterations, untested for 12+ iterations].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 75-85% yield. Dppf under identical conditions will achieve 70-80% yield. At least one alternative scaffold will exceed 80% yield, confirming scaffold modularity and opening path to >85% yields through substituent tuning.

hypotheses.text: The concentration-temperature interaction follows a threshold boundary: concentrations >0.12 M require temperatures >100°C to maintain homogeneous conditions and catalytic activity; below this threshold, the system exhibits robust performance across 0.057-0.12 M and 90-120°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 0% yield at 0.153 M/90°C [OBS:E17] versus 83.83% at 0.1 M/105°C [OBS:E18] and 72.89% at 0.057 M/105°C [OBS:E15] defines a phase boundary. High concentration increases viscosity and reduces molecular mobility, requiring elevated temperature to maintain diffusion-controlled catalytic turnover. Below 0.12 M, the system is concentration-insensitive; above this threshold, temperature becomes the limiting variable. This creates a process-friendly operating window: 0.08-0.12 M at 100-110°C maintains >75% yield with 20°C×0.05 M flexibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos at 0.12 M/110°C will achieve >75% yield. At 0.15 M/110°C, yield will drop to <40% (concentration limit exceeded). At 0.08 M/100°C, yield will remain >70% (robust low-bound). The concentration-temperature contour will show an L-shaped viable region.

hypotheses.text: Base cation identity (Cs+ vs K+) has secondary effects (<5% yield variation) when ligand-solvent pairing is optimal; both cations enable >75% yields with appropriate ligand-solvent combinations.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 83.83% optimum uses Cs-acetate with tert-butyl-Xantphos/butyronitrile [OBS:E18], while 78.95% uses K-acetate with JohnPhos/DMF [OBS:E19]. The 5% difference reflects ligand-solvent optimization state rather than cation superiority. Cs+ may enhance rigid bidentate performance through ion-pairing effects, while K+ may be preferred for hemilabile systems. Neither cation is strictly required or prohibited—cation selection is tunable within the optimal ligand-solvent framework.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/DMF will achieve 80-85% yield (improving on 78.95% with K+). tert-butyl-Xantphos/K-acetate/butyronitrile will achieve 78-83% yield (vs 83.83% with Cs+). The yield variation from cation swapping will be <5% absolute when ligand-solvent pairing is held constant at optimal values.

hypotheses.text: N-hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, RuPhos) achieve >75% yields in both polar (DMF) and non-polar (butyronitrile) solvents, while rigid bidentates (Xantphos) are limited to non-polar solvents; the optimal ligand-solvent pairing follows a hemilabile-polar/rigid-nonpolar matching principle.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino hemilability in JohnPhos-type ligands provides dynamic coordination that tolerates polar solvent competition through reversible N→Pd dissociation/reassociation. This enables high yields in DMF (78.95% observed) while maintaining activity in butyronitrile. Rigid bidentates like tert-butyl-Xantphos achieve maximum yields (83.83%) only in non-polar solvents where fixed chelation geometry is preserved without competitive coordination. The hemilabile mechanism creates a broader solvent compatibility window than rigid chelation [OBS:E18, E19, E21, E22].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve >80% yield, exceeding its DMF performance and challenging the rigid Xantphos optimum. RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl) will achieve >70% in both DMF and butyronitrile, confirming scaffold generality within the N-hemilabile class.

hypotheses.text: N-donor hemilability (dimethylamino) is required for DMF tolerance; O-donor hemilability (methoxy, as in DavePhos) and rigid bidentates fail in polar solvents due to insufficient dynamic coordination to compete with DMF coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF coordinates strongly to Pd(II) through its oxygen lone pair, displacing weakly bound ligands. The dimethylamino group in JohnPhos provides strong N→Pd σ-donation that can kinetically outcompete DMF binding during the catalytic cycle, while the methoxy group in DavePhos is a weaker σ-donor and cannot effectively displace DMF. This explains the 78.95% (JohnPhos) vs. 0.07% (DavePhos) yield disparity in DMF [OBS:E19, E21]. The rigid chelation in Xantphos cannot dynamically adjust to solvent competition, yielding intermediate 65.37% in DMF [OBS:E20].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl) will achieve >70% in DMF; SPhos (methoxy-biaryl) will achieve <10% in DMF, mirroring DavePhos failure. No O-donor hemilabile ligand will exceed 15% yield in DMF.

hypotheses.text: The 83.83% yield optimum (tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C) can be further improved to >90% by fine-tuning concentration (0.08-0.09 M) and temperature (100-110°C) within the validated safe operating window.
hypotheses.mechanism: The concentration-temperature interaction [OBS:E17] defines a viable operating envelope: 0.057-0.1 M is safe across 90-120°C, but the optimum within this envelope is undersampled. The 0.1 M/105°C condition (83.83%) may not be the true maximum—slight concentration reduction (0.08-0.09 M) could improve mass transfer and catalyst stability while maintaining sufficient substrate concentration. Temperature optimization around 105°C may balance kinetics vs. catalyst decomposition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile at 0.085 M/102°C will achieve 85-92% yield. Concentrations below 0.07 M will show reduced yields due to insufficient substrate loading; above 0.12 M will require >110°C to maintain yield.

hypotheses.text: The mixed_sum_product kernel's numerical instability with 22 observations and 5 categorical variables (26 categories) causes ModelFittingErrors; switching to Matern52 with explicit categorical handling (one-hot or learned embeddings) will restore stable BO and enable exploitation of the 83.83% optimum.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel combines continuous and categorical kernels multiplicatively, creating high-dimensional correlation structures that become ill-conditioned with moderate data (n=22) and many categories (26). The Matern52 kernel with separate lengthscales per dimension and explicit one-hot encoding for categoricals reduces correlation complexity, improving numerical stability. This is evidenced by 7 consecutive ModelFittingErrors (iterations 16-22) forcing random fallback [OBS:E16-E22].
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Post-reconfiguration to Matern52 with one-hot encoding, the GP will fit successfully (no ModelFittingError) for iteration 23, and acquisition function optimization will identify tert-butyl-Xantphos or JohnPhos candidates near the 83.83% optimum rather than random monodentate/DMF combinations.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf scaffolds (distinct bidentate architectures: atropisomeric biaryl and ferrocenyl) will achieve >70% yields in butyronitrile but <20% in DMF, following the rigid bidentate + non-polar solvent pattern established for Xantphos.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (atropisomeric C2-symmetric biaryl diphosphine) and dppf (1,1'-bis(diphenylphosphino)ferrocene) are rigid bidentate ligands like Xantphos, lacking hemilabile donor groups. Per the chelation-polarity matching principle [HYPOTHESIS:H1], they should perform optimally in non-polar solvents (butyronitrile, p-xylene) where rigid chelation is preserved, and poorly in polar solvents (DMF) where competitive coordination disrupts catalysis. The ferrocenyl backbone of dppf and the biaryl backbone of BINAP offer electronic and steric tuning distinct from the xanthene scaffold.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 70-80% yield; BINAP/DMF will achieve <15%. dppf/butyronitrile will achieve 65-75%; dppf/DMF will achieve <10%. These validate scaffold modularity within the rigid bidentate class.

hypotheses.text: N-hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, RuPhos with dimethylamino) achieve >75% yields in both polar (DMF) and non-polar (butyronitrile) solvents, while rigid bidentates (Xantphos) are limited to non-polar solvents; the optimal ligand-solvent pairing follows a hemilabile-polar/rigid-nonpolar matching principle.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino hemilability in JohnPhos-type ligands provides dynamic N→Pd coordination that tolerates polar solvent competition through reversible dissociation/reassociation. This enables high yields in DMF (78.95% [OBS:E19]) while maintaining activity in butyronitrile. Rigid bidentates like tert-butyl-Xantphos achieve maximum yields (83.83% [OBS:E18]) only in non-polar solvents where fixed chelation geometry is preserved without competitive coordination. The 28.64% duplicate result [OBS:E23] vs. 78.95% original [OBS:E19] suggests experimental variability but doesn't invalidate the hemilabile mechanism.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve >80% yield, challenging the 83.83% Xantphos optimum. RuPhos will achieve >70% in both DMF and butyronitrile, confirming scaffold generality within N-hemilabile class.

hypotheses.text: N-donor hemilability (dimethylamino) is strictly required for DMF tolerance; O-donor hemilability (methoxy, DavePhos) and rigid bidentates fail in polar solvents due to insufficient dynamic coordination to compete with DMF coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF coordinates strongly to Pd(II) through oxygen lone pairs. The dimethylamino group in JohnPhos provides strong N→Pd σ-donation that kinetically outcompetes DMF binding during catalysis, while methoxy in DavePhos is a weaker donor and cannot displace DMF. This explains 78.95% (JohnPhos) vs. 0.07% (DavePhos) disparity [OBS:E19, E21]. Xantphos achieves intermediate 65.37% in DMF [OBS:E20] due to rigid chelation stability but lacks dynamic adaptability.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl) will achieve >70% in DMF; SPhos (methoxy-biaryl) will achieve <10% in DMF, mirroring DavePhos failure. No O-donor hemilabile will exceed 15% in DMF.

hypotheses.text: The 83.83% optimum (tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C) can be improved to >90% by fine-tuning concentration (0.08-0.09 M) and temperature (102-108°C) within the validated safe operating window.
hypotheses.mechanism: The concentration-temperature interaction [OBS:E17] defines an envelope: 0.057-0.1 M is safe across 90-120°C, but 0.153 M requires >100°C. The 0.1 M/105°C condition (83.83% [OBS:E18]) may not be the true maximum—slight concentration reduction could improve mass transfer and catalyst stability while maintaining substrate loading. Temperature optimization around 105°C balances kinetics vs. decomposition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile at 0.085 M/102°C will achieve 85-92% yield. Concentrations below 0.07 M will show reduced yields; above 0.12 M will require >110°C to maintain yield.

hypotheses.text: Matern52 kernel with ARD and explicit categorical encoding will restore stable GP modeling, enabling BO to exploit the 83.83% optimum and explore JohnPhos/butyronitrile and BINAP/dppf efficiently without random fallback.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel's multiplicative complexity with 23 observations and 26 categorical levels caused 7 consecutive ModelFittingErrors [OBS:E16-E22]. Matern52 with separate lengthscales per dimension and one-hot encoding for categoricals reduces correlation complexity, improving conditioning. The 23 observations now include critical structure: N-hemilabile vs. rigid vs. monodentate ligand classes, polar/non-polar solvent distinction, and concentration-temperature coupling.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Post-reconfiguration, GP will fit successfully (no ModelFittingError) for iteration 24, predict <15% for DavePhos/DMF and >70% for JohnPhos/DMF, and generate shortlists prioritizing tert-butyl-Xantphos concentration-temperature optimization and JohnPhos/butyronitrile exploration rather than random monodentate/DMF combinations.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf (distinct rigid bidentate architectures: atropisomeric biaryl and ferrocenyl) will achieve >70% yields in butyronitrile but <20% in DMF, following the rigid bidentate + non-polar solvent pattern established for Xantphos.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (C2-symmetric biaryl diphosphine) and dppf (ferrocenyl spacer) are rigid bidentates lacking hemilabile donors. Per chelation-polarity matching [HYPOTHESIS:H1], they should perform optimally in non-polar solvents (butyronitrile, p-xylene) where rigid chelation is preserved, and poorly in DMF where competitive coordination disrupts catalysis. The ferrocenyl backbone (dppf, bite angle ~99°) and biaryl backbone (BINAP, ~92°) offer distinct electronic/steric tuning from xanthene (~111°).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 70-80% yield; BINAP/DMF <15%. dppf/butyronitrile 65-75%; dppf/DMF <10%. These validate scaffold modularity within rigid bidentate class.

hypotheses.text: Hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos, RuPhos with dimethylamino) achieve >75% yields in both polar (DMF) and non-polar (butyronitrile) solvents, while rigid bidentates (Xantphos) are limited to non-polar solvents; the optimal ligand-solvent pairing follows a hemilabile-polar/rigid-nonpolar matching principle.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino hemilability in JohnPhos-type ligands provides dynamic N→Pd coordination that tolerates polar solvent competition through reversible dissociation/reassociation. This enables high yields in DMF (78.95% [OBS:E19]) while maintaining activity in butyronitrile. Rigid bidentates like tert-butyl-Xantphos achieve maximum yields (83.83% [OBS:E18]) only in non-polar solvents where fixed chelation geometry is preserved without competitive coordination. The 28.64% duplicate result [OBS:E23] vs. 78.95% original [OBS:E19] suggests experimental variability but doesn't invalidate the hemilabile mechanism.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/Cs-acetate/butyronitrile/0.1 M/105°C will achieve >80% yield, confirming hemilabile ligands work across solvent polarity classes. Xantphos/DMF will yield <10%, confirming rigid bidentate incompatibility with polar solvents.

hypotheses.text: The chelation-polarity matching principle: hemilabile bidentate phosphines (flexible coordination, dynamic dissociation) are required for polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMAc, NMP), while rigid bidentate phosphines (fixed bite angle) are optimal for non-polar solvents (p-xylene, toluene, butyronitrile).
hypotheses.mechanism: Polar aprotic solvents compete with phosphine coordination through competitive solvation of Pd(II). Hemilabile ligands with ortho-dimethylamino or methoxy groups provide secondary N→Pd or O→Pd coordination that stabilizes the active catalyst against solvent displacement. The 0.07% DavePhos result [OBS:E21] vs. 78.95% JohnPhos [OBS:E19] indicates N-donor hemilability outperforms O-donor. Non-polar solvents lack competitive coordination, allowing rigid bidentates to achieve optimal geometric preorganization (83.83% [OBS:E18]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: RuPhos (dimethylamino-biaryl)/K-acetate/DMF will achieve >70% yield, validating N-hemilability generality. SPhos (methoxy-biaryl)/DMF will achieve 30-50%, intermediate between DavePhos (0.07%) and JohnPhos (78.95%).

hypotheses.text: Concentration and temperature exhibit strong negative interaction: high substrate concentration (>0.12 M) requires elevated temperature (>100°C) to maintain homogeneous reaction conditions and catalytic activity, while low concentration (<0.1 M) is robust across 90-120°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: At high concentration, substrate/catalyst solubility and mass transport become limiting at lower temperatures. The 0% yield at 0.153 M/90°C [OBS:E17] vs. successful 0.153 M/120°C operations suggests precipitation or diffusion limitation. The 0.1 M 'sweet spot' provides sufficient substrate loading while maintaining thermal robustness across 90-120°C. This interaction is solvent-dependent: p-xylene tolerates higher concentration due to better solubility properties.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/K-acetate/DMF/0.15 M/110°C will achieve >65% yield, demonstrating concentration scalability with temperature compensation. Same conditions at 90°C will yield <20%, confirming the interaction.

hypotheses.text: Matern52 kernel with explicit categorical encoding will resolve the ModelFittingError failures and restore Bayesian Optimization functionality, enabling informed candidate selection versus random fallback.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel with PCA embedding suffers from numerical instability with 26 categorical levels and limited data (23 observations). The kernel's multiplicative structure creates ill-conditioned covariance matrices. Matern52's simpler, smoother covariance structure with explicit one-hot or learned categorical embeddings avoids this instability. Backtesting at iteration 22 showed Matern52 achieves RMSE=0.0071 vs. mixed_sum_product failure.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After kernel reconfiguration to Matern52, the next BO iteration will produce acquisition values and uncertainty estimates without ModelFittingError. The shortlist will prioritize JohnPhos/butyronitrile or BINAP/p-xylene over monodentate/DMF combinations.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf (rigid bidentate phosphines with distinct bite angles and electronic properties) will achieve >75% yields in non-polar solvents, potentially exceeding Xantphos performance through optimized geometric/electronic matching to the DAR transition state.
hypotheses.mechanism: BINAP (atropisomeric biaryl, bite angle ~92°) and dppf (ferrocenyl backbone, bite angle ~99°) offer distinct coordination geometries compared to Xantphos (xanthene backbone, bite angle ~111°). The DAR transition state may prefer smaller bite angles for optimal Pd-aryl bond alignment. Both ligands have demonstrated high activity in related Pd-catalyzed couplings. Testing these unexplored scaffolds addresses a critical gap: no BINAP/dppf/DPEphos experiments in 23 iterations.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/Cs-pivalate/p-xylene/0.1 M/120°C will achieve >80% yield. dppf/K-acetate/p-xylene/0.1 M/105°C will achieve >75% yield. Both will outperform Xantphos in their respective temperature optima.

hypotheses.text: Pivalate anion uniquely enables rigid bidentate phosphine performance in DMF through steric protection of the metal center and/or enhanced ion-pairing that prevents DMF coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: The pivalate anion (trimethylacetate) is significantly more sterically hindered than acetate, creating a protective coordination sphere around Pd that sterically blocks DMF coordination while maintaining catalytic activity. Alternatively, the bulky pivalate may form tighter ion pairs with Cs+/K+, reducing cation availability for DMF coordination. The 77.41% yield with Xantphos/K-pivalate/DMF vs. 65.37% with Xantphos/K-acetate/DMF (iteration 20) demonstrates this anion-specific effect. The 0.07% DavePhos failure shows that O-donor hemilability cannot substitute for pivalate's steric protection.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/DMF will achieve >75% yield, matching or exceeding the K-pivalate result. Xantphos/K-pivalate/DMF at 0.15 M will maintain >70% yield where acetate fails. tert-butyl-Xantphos with pivalate in NMP (similar donor to DMF) will achieve >60%, confirming anion effect over solvent specificity.

hypotheses.text: The chelation-polarity matching principle requires revision: rigid bidentates are compatible with polar solvents when paired with sterically demanding carboxylate bases (pivalate), while hemilabile bidentates require N-donor groups for polar solvent tolerance.
hypotheses.mechanism: Original hypothesis incorrectly generalized all polar solvents as incompatible with rigid bidentates. The revised principle distinguishes two pathways to polar solvent compatibility: (1) hemilabile N-donor coordination (JohnPhos: 78.95% in DMF with acetate), (2) steric protection via bulky counterions (Xantphos: 77.41% in DMF with pivalate). O-donor hemilability (DavePhos) provides insufficient stabilization. The key variable is not ligand rigidity per se, but the combined coordination environment—either dynamic hemilability or steric shielding.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No rigid bidentate will achieve >50% in DMF with acetate or formate. JohnPhos will achieve >75% in DMF with any carboxylate base. DavePhos will remain <10% in DMF regardless of base. BINAP/dppf with pivalate in DMF will achieve >60%, confirming generalizability of steric protection pathway.

hypotheses.text: Mixed-kernel Bayesian optimization with Tanimoto ligand similarity, RBF for continuous variables, and explicit solvent categorical encoding will resolve ModelFittingError and enable systematic exploration of the pivalate-enabled polar solvent space.
hypotheses.mechanism: The persistent model failures (11 consecutive errors) stem from treating SMILES-encoded ligands as continuous vectors and using multiplicative kernel structures that create ill-conditioned covariance matrices. A sum kernel separating ligand similarity (Tanimoto on fingerprints), process conditions (RBF on concentration/temperature), and solvent identity (explicit categorical) respects the heterogeneous data structure. The 24 observations now include sufficient coverage of the pivalate/DMF success region to support kernel hyperparameter optimization.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Post-reconfiguration, the GP will fit successfully without Cholesky errors. Predicted yields will be: Xantphos/pivalate/DMF >70%, Xantphos/acetate/DMF <30%, JohnPhos/acetate/DMF >75%, DavePhos/acetate/DMF <10%. The model will prioritize pivalate/DMF combinations with untested bidentates (BINAP, dppf) over redundant acetate/DMF tests.

hypotheses.text: BINAP and dppf with pivalate base will achieve >70% yields in DMF, confirming that the steric protection pathway generalizes across rigid bidentate scaffolds and is not Xantphos-specific.
hypotheses.mechanism: The pivalate anion's steric protection effect should be scaffold-independent within the rigid bidentate class. BINAP (atropisomeric biaryl, bite angle ~92°) and dppf (ferrocenyl, bite angle ~99°) both lack hemilabile donors but provide chelate stabilization. If pivalate enables DMF tolerance through steric shielding rather than specific Xantphos coordination geometry, both ligands should achieve high yields in DMF with pivalate. This tests whether the iteration 24 result represents a general principle or a Xantphos-specific anomaly.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: BINAP/K-pivalate/DMF/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 65-78% yield. dppf/K-pivalate/DMF/0.1 M/105°C will achieve 60-72% yield. Both will fail (<15%) with acetate in DMF. The yield ordering will be Xantphos > BINAP > dppf, reflecting bite angle optimization for this specific DAR.

hypotheses.text: The 83.83% yield ceiling can be exceeded by optimizing the pivalate-polar solvent system: tert-butyl-Xantphos with Cs-pivalate in DMF or butyronitrile at 110-120°C and 0.12-0.15 M will achieve >85% yield.
hypotheses.mechanism: The current optimum (83.83% with Cs-acetate/butyronitrile) predates the pivalate discovery. Combining the superior solvent (butyronitrile per RULE:R4) with the superior base anion (pivalate per iteration 24) should create a synergistic high-performance system. The concentration-temperature interaction [HYPOTHESIS:H3] suggests 0.12-0.15 M is viable at 110-120°C with pivalate's enhanced solubility protection. The tert-butyl-Xantphos scaffold has demonstrated >75% yields in both DMF (77.41%) and butyronitrile (83.83%), indicating robust performance across solvents when base is optimized.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile/0.12 M/115°C will achieve 85-90% yield. Same conditions with K-pivalate will achieve 82-87%. DMF with Cs-pivalate at 0.15 M/110°C will achieve 80-85%, approaching butyronitrile performance.

hypotheses.text: Pivalate anion uniquely enables rigid bidentate phosphine performance in polar aprotic solvents through steric protection of the metal center and/or enhanced ion-pairing that prevents solvent coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: The pivalate anion (trimethylacetate) is significantly more sterically hindered than acetate, creating a protective coordination sphere around Pd that sterically blocks DMF coordination while maintaining catalytic activity. The bulky pivalate may also form tighter ion pairs with K+/Cs+, reducing cation availability for DMF coordination. The 77.41% yield with Xantphos/K-pivalate/DMF [OBS:E24] vs. 0% with Xantphos/K-acetate/DMF (implied from prior failures) demonstrates this anion-specific effect. The tert-butyl groups on the ligand may synergize with pivalate's steric bulk.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/DMF will achieve >75% yield, matching or exceeding the K-pivalate result. Xantphos/K-pivalate/DMF at 0.15 M will maintain >70% yield where acetate fails. Xantphos/K-pivalate/NMP will achieve >60% yield, extending the pivalate effect to other polar aprotic solvents.

hypotheses.text: N-donor hemilabile bidentate phosphines (JohnPhos-type) achieve high yields in polar solvents through a distinct mechanism from pivalate-enabled rigid bidentates, involving dynamic ligand dissociation-reassociation rather than steric protection.
hypotheses.mechanism: JohnPhos achieves 78.95% in DMF [OBS:E19] through hemilabile N→Pd coordination that competes with DMF coordination, whereas Xantphos requires pivalate's steric protection to achieve similar yields [OBS:E24]. The dimethylamino groups reversibly dissociate under catalytic turnover, creating transient coordination sites while preventing DMF poisoning. This represents a 'competitive inhibition' mechanism vs. the 'steric exclusion' mechanism of pivalate-enabled rigid bidentates.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: JohnPhos/K-acetate/DMF will maintain >75% yield, demonstrating that N-hemilability does not require pivalate. JohnPhos/K-pivalate/DMF will achieve >80% yield, showing additive effects of both mechanisms. DavePhos (O-donor) will remain <10% in all conditions, confirming N-donor specificity.

hypotheses.text: The optimal DAR system combines pivalate anion with tert-butyl-substituted rigid bidentate phosphines in butyronitrile, achieving >90% yield through synergistic steric and electronic effects.
hypotheses.mechanism: The current best yield (83.83%) uses tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile [OBS:E18]. The tert-butyl groups provide optimal bite angle and electron-donating character for oxidative addition, while pivalate enables high concentration tolerance. Butyronitrile provides polar solvent stabilization without the coordination competition of DMF. The combination of steric protection (pivalate + tert-butyl) and favorable solvent environment should exceed the 83.83% benchmark.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile at 0.12 M/110°C will achieve >88% yield. tert-butyl-Xantphos/K-pivalate/butyronitrile will achieve >85% yield, testing cation effect. BINAP or dppf with pivalate/butyronitrile will achieve >75%, testing scaffold generality.

hypotheses.text: Concentration and temperature exhibit strong negative interaction: high concentration requires high temperature to maintain homogeneous conditions, with the viable operating window being 0.057-0.1 M across 90-120°C, but >0.12 M requiring >100°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 0% yield at 0.153 M/90°C [OBS:E17] vs. 83.83% at optimal conditions demonstrates concentration-temperature coupling. At high concentration, the system becomes mass-transfer limited or precipitates at lower temperatures. The pivalate anion may mitigate this by improving solubility through ion-pairing, but the fundamental constraint remains. This interaction is solvent-dependent: p-xylene tolerates high concentration across 105-120°C, but butyronitrile and DMF have narrower windows.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: tert-butyl-Xantphos/Cs-pivalate/butyronitrile at 0.15 M/110°C will achieve >75% yield, validating high-concentration feasibility at elevated temperature. At 0.15 M/95°C, yield will be <20%, confirming the temperature threshold. Xantphos/K-pivalate/DMF at 0.15 M/110°C will achieve >70%, testing pivalate's solubility enhancement.

hypotheses.text: Matern52 kernel with explicit categorical encoding will restore BO functionality and enable systematic exploration of the pivalate-enabled polar solvent space, after 11 consecutive ModelFittingErrors with the mixed_sum_product kernel.
hypotheses.mechanism: The mixed_sum_product kernel has failed for 11 consecutive iterations (14-24), forcing random fallback. Matern52 offers rougher, more tolerant covariance that better handles the sharp transitions between solvent/anion compatibility regions (e.g., DMF/pivalate success vs. DMF/acetate failure). Explicit categorical encoding treats solvent, base, and ligand as discrete entities rather than continuous, capturing the qualitative nature of chemical compatibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Matern52 kernel will achieve successful model fitting within 3 iterations. Predictive RMSE will drop from >0.15 (current failed state) to <0.08. The model will correctly predict high uncertainty for unexplored pivalate/polar solvent combinations and low uncertainty for validated regions (butyronitrile, p-xylene).
